data.title: Intel board should consider 'radical change' with new CEO: analysts
data.description:
Intel's (NASDAQ:INTC) announcement that Pat Gelsinger stepped down from leading the semiconductor giant should be followed up with "radical change," some in the investment community believe.

"While INTC's underperformance during Gelsinger's tenure as CEO was worse than that of INTC's previous four CEOs, INTC's stock hasn't outperformed the [Philadelphia Semiconductor Index] under any of them and only under Brian Krzanich's leadership did INTC beat the S&P 500," Truist analyst William Stein wrote in a note to clients. "We credit Gelsinger for making some significant changes at the company, but in our view he didn't move fast enough or go far enough."

Prior to Gelsinger, Intel was led by Craig Barrett from 1997 to 2005; Paul Otellini from 2005 to 2013; Brian Krzanich from 2013 to 2019; and Bob Swan from 2019 to 2021.

Problems go back decades

Intel's challenges go back decades and while the company has kept up its strength in manufacturing, that dominance led to a "culture of entitlement" that led to it missing out on seismic shifts in technology, including mobile and AI-centric compute, Stein added.

When the Santa Clara, Calif.-based Intel lost its manufacturing superiority to Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) around 2017, this ultimately revealed its "less-than-perfect" design choices, Stein added, as evidenced by the fact its revenue per employee is below competitors Nvidia (NVDA), Broadcom (AVGO), Taiwan Semiconductor, AMD (AMD) and GlobalFoundries (GFS).

"In retrospect, it all feels like Gelsinger tried to do too many things without clearly addressing the toughest thing: culture," Stein wrote. "This may have been a huge focus, but it certainly wasn't clear to us. Another CEO who tries to improve execution of Gelsinger's strategy seems unlikely, and we recall the challenges INTC had finding a CEO in each of the last two searches. We think INTC needs a more radical change."

Hamstrung?

Aside from Gelsinger's vision, of which the board may have lost confidence in, the company may be hamstrung via the U.S. CHIPs Act.

Last week, the Biden administration finalized a $7.86B grant to the company to boost domestic manufacturing, down from a previously announced $8.5B. However, there is a provision that prevents Intel from selling off its Intel Foundry Services unit and forces it to maintain a controlling stake.

This may have resulted in Gelsinger's "forced retirement," Seeking Alpha analyst Michael Del Monte said via email.

"The board cited that they have lost confidence in Mr. Gelsinger turning Intel around," Del Monte added.

New opportunities?

Intel's problems are not going to be fixed anytime soon, Mizuho analyst Vijay Rakesh said. It is a "distant third" to Nvidia and AMD in AI server adoption; the server CPU market is slowing and Intel is losing share to AMD; it has a nascent foundry business; and there are continued problems with AI ramps.

However, new leadership could give the company a new vision, even as these issues remain, Rakesh added. He kept his Neutral rating and $23 price target on Intel as it remains "as a key player in traditional PC/Server markets," even as it lags in AI.

data.title: ASML rises as company says new export curb control rules won't impact guidance
data.description:
ASML (NASDAQ:ASML) shares rose 3.5% on Monday as the company said the new semiconductor export curb control rules enacted by the Biden administration would not impact previously issued financial guidance for next year.

"For 2024, we do not expect any direct material impact on our business," ASML said in a statement. "For 2025, we expect that the impact will fall within what was communicated at the time of our Q3 2024 earnings, namely that we expect 2025 total net sales to be between €30 billion and €35 billion and that we expect our China business (net system sales plus net service and field option sales) to be around 20% of our total net sales for that full year."

ASML added that long-term demand scenarios will not be impacted by the new regulations, as these scenarios are based on global demand.

"We therefore also confirm potential 2030 scenarios for annual total net sales between approximately €44 billion and €60 billion as outlined during our Investor Day on November 14, 2024," ASML explained.

The semiconductor equipment firm also noted it is "fully committed" to complying with all applicable laws and regulations, including export control legislation in the countries it operates in.

Shares of other semiconductor equipment firms were also higher on Monday, including Applied Materials (AMAT), Lam Research (LRCX) and KLA Corp. (KLAC).

Lam Research also said it does not expect the new export controls to impact previously issued guidance.

On Monday, the U.S. Department of Commerce placed additional curbs on China’s access to vital tools and components for AI chips. The new rules restrict sales of 27 types of high-bandwidth memory and chipmaking equipment, including products made outside the country by American companies, and are in addition to existing curbs affecting advanced logic chips.

The Chinese Ministry of Commerce called the U.S. restrictions “economic coercion,” and “seriously” threatening to the global supply chain. It said the country will take measures to safeguard its interests, but did not disclose what these measures would be.

Seeking Alpha's Preeti Singh contributed to this story.

(This story has been updated to include Lam Research's comment.)

data.title: Main Street Capital snaps six straight days of gains
data.description:
Main Street Capital (NYSE:MAIN) shares snapped six straight sessions of gains, as the stock closed 0.20% lower, at $55.36 on Monday.

The Texas-based private equity firm gained 4.31% in the preceding six trading days. The stock has gained more than 27% so far this year, slightly outperforming the broader S&P 500 Index, which has gained 26%.

MAIN is up 8% over the past one month. The stock closed 0.85% higher, at $55.47 on Friday.

Main Street Capital (MAIN) shares gained last month after delivering third quarter earnings that topped the average analyst estimate, as the business development company ended the three-month period with attractive investment pipelines in both its lower middle market and private loan investment strategies.

The company reported Q3 distributable net investment income per share of $1.06, exceeding the $1.01 average analyst estimate.

Seeking Alpha analyst, Constantine Theophylactou believes Main Street Capital is a top-performing BDC with strong financials, portfolio diversification, and consistent dividend coverage, but added, that MAIN trades at a premium to NAV, indicating overvaluation.

“Trading at a 68.5% premium to NAV and with a high price-to-book ratio compared to peers, MAIN appears overvalued at its present levels,” Theophylactou said.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating, SA authors, and sell-side analysts, all agree with the view that the stock is currently a “HOLD”.

data.title: Nasdaq, S&P, and Dow finished mixed as Treasury yield inversion talks resumed
data.description:
Traders and investors on Wall Street watched as the major market averages close out Monday’s session in a mixed fashion as the financial community digested the latest comments from President-elect Donald Trump as he threatened to implement more trade tariffs.

The tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was the best performer as it advanced 0.9%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) ended Monday higher by 0.2% and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) observed a loss as it handed back 0.2%.

From a sector-by-sector point of view, just three of the 11 S&P segments closed in positive territory. Leading the way higher was the Communication Services space. At the other end of the spectrum, Utilities and Real Estate were the two worst sector performers.

"While broad equity indices continued their march higher today, the strength was concentrated in select sectors like communication services and information technology. With economically sensitive sectors like oil & gas, real estate, and utilities closing firmly in the red, investors were fleeing back into larger capitalization market generals - signaling risk-off behavior," Seeking Alpha’s Ahan Vashi of The Quantamental Investor investing group stated.

"Given historically elevated equity market valuations, prudent investors must remain highly selective and continue to manage risk proactively," Vashi added.

U.S. President-elect Donald Trump on Saturday threatened the BRICS member nations with 100% tariffs if they moved to create a new currency to replace the U.S. dollar. "As ever with Trump it seems to be a shot across the bows but likely wouldn't ultimately be great for the U.S. economy if implemented," Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid said.

U.S. Treasury yields took a step higher to start the week and yield curve inversion talks also returned as the US2Y and US10Y both tagged 4.20%.

For the day, the shorter end more rate sensitive U.S. 2-Year Treasury yield (US2Y) ticked up by 2 basis points to 4.18% and the longer end U.S. 10-Year Treasury yield (US10Y) advanced by 1 basis point to 4.19%.

For more, see how Treasury yields have done across the curve on the Seeking Alpha bond page.

On the economic calendar front, U.S. manufacturing PMI rose more than expected in November to 49.7 compared with the November flash print of 48.8 and 48.5 print in October.

As for stocks that were on the move, shares of Super Micro Computer (SMCI) surged 28.6%. See what some of the bulls and bears on SMCI have to say about the company.

Shares of Intel (INTC) closed lower after an early day rally following the surprising announcement that Chief Executive Officer Pat Gelsinger is retiring and will be replaced by co-CEOs David Zinsner and Michelle Johnston Holthaus.

Moreover, both Roku (ROKU) and Trade Desk (TTD) popped 11.6% and 5.1%, respectively as Guggenheim suggested that a hypothetical merger between the two could work to each other's strengths and ambitions.

data.title: Southern Copper upgraded at J.P. Morgan on strong copper proxy appeal
data.description:
Southern Copper (NYSE:SCCO) -0.3% in Monday's trading despite winning an upgrade at J.P. Morgan to Neutral from Underweight with a $92.50 price target, raised from $68, citing the addition of the Tia Maria project into estimates coupled with a positive outlook on copper prices.

Southern Copper (SCCO) has always been pricier than peers, historically trading at 9x-10x EV/EBITDA, while peers typically trade at half of that, which JPM's Rodolfo Angele says is explained by the fact that the company is a very good proxy for copper, which comprises ~85% of total revenues, one of the highest exposures to copper available in a miner.

The company's performance is closely tied to the price and demand for copper - a key industrial metal used across various sectors - so Southern Copper's (SCCO) premium is justified because investors that want to get exposure to the metal use the stock as a proxy, almost like a copper ETF, Angele says.

data.title: Top quant-rated global financial stocks: KINS, ROOT, SUPV
data.description:
Global financials continue to head the triple momentum ranking, according to BofA Securities.

Triple momentum encompasses earnings momentum, price momentum, and news momentum.

Analysts explain that earnings momentum monitors the changing consensus of earnings estimates and is defined “as the three-month change in the consensus EPS estimate for each stock.”

Price Momentum is the sentiment of market participants buying and selling equities and is defined “as the slope of the log-linear regression using daily prices over the preceding 12-months.”

Finally, news momentum captures “the sentiment from all other market players including journalists, experts, regulators, corporates, and more.”

The global sectors ranking highest in triple momentum are insurance (KIE), diversified financials (VFH), and banks (KBE).

Here are the highest-ranking global stocks in insurance, diversified financials, and banks, according to Seeking Alpha quant metrics:

  • Kingstone Companies Inc. (KINS) – Quant rating: 4.99
  • Root Inc. (ROOT) – Quant rating: 4.98

data.title: Lion Electric surges after getting a temporary reprieve from its creditors
data.description:
Canadian electric truck and bus maker Lion Electric (NYSE:LEV) jumped 55% after winning a temporary reprieve from its creditors. The company also said it would lay off 400 of its employees in Canada and the U.S.

Lion Electric also announced that it will suspend manufacturing operations at its facility located in Joliet, Illinois, as a result of the workforce reduction, according to a statement on Monday.

Separately, Bloomberg reported that Mach Capital, the investment unit of Canadian real estate developer Group Mach, is in talks to provide funding to Lion Electric (LEV). Mach will only invest if members of Montreal's Saputo family are also willing to give more cash to try to save the EV maker, a person familiar told Bloomberg.

Mach’s interest was reported earlier by the Journal de Montreal.

data.title: Nvidia competitor Tensorrent raises $693M in Series D funding round
data.description:
• Canadian startup Tenstorrent has raised $693M in a new Series D funding round as it looks to compete with Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) and others in the artificial intelligence accelerator market.
  • Tensorrent, which chose Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) last year to build its AI chips, raised funding from Samsung, Amazon co-founder Jeff Bezos and a host of others. Law firm Wilson Sonsini Goodrich & Rosati and investment firm Barclays helped with the funding round, Tensorrent Chief Operating Officer Keith Witek said.
  • The new funding values Tensorrent at $2.6B, Bloomberg reported.
  • Some of Tenstorrent's chips use RISC-V technology, an alternative to Arm (ARM) -based architecture that is being championed by Qualcomm (QCOM) and some other semiconductor companies. The other major semiconductor architecture is x86, which is championed by Intel (INTC) and AMD (AMD).

data.title: Fed's Waller leans toward December rate cut, sees more cuts over next year
data.description:
• Federal Reserve Governor Christopher Waller expects rate cuts to continue over the next year "until we approach a more neutral setting of the policy rate," he said in prepared text for a speech at the American Institute for Economic Research on Monday.
  • Furthermore, he said he leans toward supporting a cut at the Federal Open Market Committee's December meeting based on economic data at hand today.
  • He said forecasts still show that inflation will continue toward the Fed's 2% goal over the medium term.
  • Breaking... check back for updates.

data.title: NextEra Partners powers higher as Morgan Stanley double upgrades
data.description:
NextEra Energy Partners (NYSE:NEP) +13.6% in Monday's trading after Morgan Stanley double upgrades the clean energy company to Overweight from Underweight with a $22 price target, saying the stock has overly discounted negative outcomes of its ongoing strategic review.

"We see an attractive entry point for NEP in the recent selloff to position ahead of a potential near-term catalyst: the resolution of its strategic review, perhaps as soon as December or January," Morgan Stanley's Robert Kad writes.

The analyst says NextEra Partners (NEP) now trades below its intrinsic value across likely outcomes of the strategic review following the stock's 17% decline since reporting a surprise Q3 net loss, while he currently models a 50% distribution cut in Q4, an expectation he says is now more than adequately reflected in the stock price.

More broadly, "demand for renewables over the long-term is more durable than is currently appreciated by the market," according to Kad, saying that as demand for energy rises, given drivers such as data center growth and penetration of electric vehicles, "even in the absence of tax credits, there will still be demand for renewables, albeit potentially at a more modest level, given the underlying power demand trends."

Kad maintains Buy ratings for Clearway Energy (CWEN) and HA Sustainable Infrastructure (HASI), which he says will remain well positioned to capture growth in contracted renewable investments but may trade sideways awaiting clarity on potential revisions to the Inflation Reduction Act.
